coredata.dc:title: A novel carbon-nanodots-based theranostic nano-drug delivery system for mitochondria-targeted imaging and glutathione-activated delivering camptothecin

coredata.dc:description: Chemotherapy is severely limited by continuously decreased therapeutic efficacy and uncontrolled side effects on normal tissue, which can be improved by constructing a nanoparticle-based drug delivery system (DDS). Nevertheless, no studies have reported on DDS-based on carbon-nanodots (CNDs), combining subcellular organelle-targeted imaging/drug delivery, high drug loading content, and glutathione (GSH)-sensitive drug release into one system. Herein, the as-fabricated CNDs can be covalently conjugated with a mitochondria-targeting ligand (triphenylphosphine, TPP), a smart GSH-responsive disulfide linker (S-S), and the anticancer drug (camptothecin, CPT) to initially prepare a theranostic nano-DDS (TPP-CNDs-S-CPT) with the drug loading efficiency of 64.6 wt%. Owing to excellent water dispersibility, superior fluorescence properties, satisfactory cell permeability, and favorable biocompatibility, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT was successfully used for intracellular mitochondrial-targeted imaging in vitro. High intracellular GSH concentrations in tumor cells caused the cleavage of S-S, resulting in concomitant activation and release of CPT, as well as significant fluorescence enhancement. In vivo, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT exhibited lower biological toxicity and even higher tumor-activatable performance than free CPT, as well as specific cancer therapy with few side effects. The mitochondria-targeted ability and the precise drug-release in tumor make TPP-CNDs-S-CPT a hopeful chemotherapy prodrug, providing significant theoretical basis and data support for in-depth understanding and exploration of chemotherapeutic DDS-based on CNDs.

TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is composed of carbon-nanodots-supported with surface modifications of mitochondria-targeting TPP and GSH stimulus-responding CPT. (b) After endocytosis, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can actively target enrichment in mitochondria and then release CPT at high concentration of GSH to cause tumor cells death.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemotherapy is severely limited by continuously decreased therapeutic efficacy and uncontrolled side effects on normal tissue, which can be improved by constructing a nanoparticle-based drug delivery system (DDS). Nevertheless, no studies have reported on DDS-based on carbon-nanodots (CNDs), combining subcellular organelle-targeted imaging/drug delivery, high drug loading content, and glutathione (GSH)-sensitive drug release into one system. Herein, the as-fabricated CNDs can be covalently conjugated with a mitochondria-targeting ligand (triphenylphosphine, TPP), a smart GSH-responsive disulfide linker (S-S), and the anticancer drug (camptothecin, CPT) to initially prepare a theranostic nano-DDS (TPP-CNDs-S-CPT) with the drug loading efficiency of 64.6 wt%. Owing to excellent water dispersibility, superior fluorescence properties, satisfactory cell permeability, and favorable biocompatibility, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT was successfully used for intracellular mitochondrial-targeted imaging in vitro. High intracellular GSH concentrations in tumor cells caused the cleavage of S-S, resulting in concomitant activation and release of CPT, as well as significant fluorescence enhancement. In vivo, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT exhibited lower biological toxicity and even higher tumor-activatable performance than free CPT, as well as specific cancer therapy with few side effects. The mitochondria-targeted ability and the precise drug-release in tumor make TPP-CNDs-S-CPT a hopeful chemotherapy prodrug, providing significant theoretical basis and data support for in-depth understanding and exploration of chemotherapeutic DDS-based on CNDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, caused by the absence of proper regulation and infinite metastases, is a leading factor of mortality throughout the world [1,2]. Various approaches, including chemotherapy [3], photothermal therapy [4], radiotherapy [5], photodynamic therapy [6], immunotherapy [7], chemodynamic therapy [8], and gene therapy [9] have all been developed to treat cancer. Clinically, chemotherapy remains a front-line and dominant treatment modality against cancer combined with surgery. However, chemotherapy often has severe toxic side effects owing to the small-molecule morphology of these chemotherapeutic drugs, leading to dispersion throughout the body without selectivity when using the traditional administration route. The other main challenge of chemotherapy is the selective removal of tumor cells without influencing normal tissues to significantly improve the utilization rate of drug and curative effect. Development of the controlled drug delivery system (DDS) that could be triggered by the picturesque tumor microenvironment (TME), is a promising method to control drug concentration at safe levels in healthy tissues, simultaneously reducing the side effects and enabling an increase in therapeutic efficacy [3,10,11]. Among some redox-sensitive DDSs, the disulfide bond (S-S) has attracted attention based on its instability under the reductive glutathione (GSH). GSH can be used as a trigger for the targeted intracellular delivery of drugs, mainly due to: (i) in living cells, the GSH concentration in intracellular (2–10 mM) was reported much higher than that in extracellular (2–20 µM), preventing premature drug leakage in the extracellular environment [12,13]; (ii) the concentration of GSH in some cancer cells has been proven several times higher than that in normal cells, increasing the release rate of the drug in cancer cells [13,14]; so, some GSH-activable DDSs with “S-S” linkage have been successfully prepared [3,15]. Based on a mitochondria-targeted nano-chemotherapeutic drug, further the introduction of the GSH-responsive drug release property will help to improve the efficacy of nano-chemotherapeutic drugs, reduce biological toxicity and side effects, and further broaden the application range of chemotherapy in the field of cancer therapy.

Camptothecin (CPT), a well-known monoterpenoid indole alkaloid and an inhibitor of topoisomerase I with antitumor activity, is produced from plants and endophytes and can trigger cell death by provoking DNA damage [3,16,17]. Tumors with a high index of proliferation are more sensitive to CPT and its analogs, so the chemotherapeutic drug CPT has significant anticancer activity against a broad spectrum of solid tumors in vitro and in vivo [18,19]. However, CPT shows some major pharmacological limitations that hamper its clinical application, including poor water solubility, low stability of the lactone ring, and severe dose-dependent systemic toxicities [18–20]. Constructing smart drug delivery system is an effective strategy to resolve these issues, and can better meet clinical needs. Different nanomedicines, including polymer-drug conjugates [3], nanoparticles [20], micelles [21], hydrogel [17], and vesicles [22], have been utilized for the delivery of CPT to reduce its toxicity and promote its properties. Whereas, majority of these carriers do not adhere to formulation requirements like high drug loading efficiency, controlled size and drug release kinetics, easy production from gram to kilogram scale, and sufficient dispersity stability in biological media, which are critical for clinical translation [23]. Recently, carbon-nanodots (CNDs) have emerged as a promising class of imaging agents and drug carriers for various biomedical applications owing to their excellent excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) emission, good dispersibility in water, easy surface modification, low toxicity, high cell membrane permeability, and outstanding drug loading capacity [24–26]. Although surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxylic groups, are amenable to CNDs conjugation with targeted moieties and drugs, very few studies have reported on nano-DDS based on CNDs with the aim of maximizing therapeutic reaction and minimizing collateral damage [27,28]. Nano-DDS combining CNDs with mitochondria-targeting moiety, GSH-activatable disulfide bond linkage, and anticancer drug CPT possesses significant advantages in boosting targeting ability, improving curative effect, and reducing side effects, but this has never been reported.

In the present study, we design a novel mitochondria-targeting and GSH stimuli-responsive nano-DDS based on triphenylphosphine (TPP), a disulfide linker (“S-S”), and fluorescent CNDs for targeted delivery of the anticancer drug CPT to mitochondria, as demonstrated in Scheme 1. We used citric acid and ethylenediamine as reaction precursors to fabricate nitrogen-doped CNDs via a hydrothermal method. CNDs, a fluorescent indicator, can monitor TPP-CNDs nano-diagnostics and locate inside cancer cells, especially mitochondria-targeting effect. Moreover, the theranostic nano-DDS TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (Scheme 1) is composed of TPP-CNDs and CPT by an “S-S”, which are served as the fluorescence reporter, mitochondrial locator, and the activable anticancer drug, respectively. The constructed theranostic nano-DDS affords five prominent characteristics: (i) efficient delivery of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT into mitochondria to improve targeting capability; (ii) controlled release of active CPT by cleaving an “S-S” under biologically abundant GSH; (iii) remarkable turn-on of blue fluorescence along with GSH-induced release of active CPT; (iv) synergistic damage of DNA in nucleus and mitochondria to promote the lysis of tumor cells; (v) significant improvement of the tumor growth inhibition rates with lower toxic and side effects. To our knowledge, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is the first CNDs-supported theranostic nano-DDS implemented for mitochondria-targetable imaging and glutathione-responsive delivery of CPT for antitumor therapy in living animal models. TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be utilized as a prodrug upon GSH-induced cleavage of an “S-S” in a drug-delivery system and high selectivity for tumor tissue by high-performance EPR effect and excellent mitochondrial targeting capability, making TPP-CNDs-S-CPT a promising nano-DDS with specific targeting ability, high therapeutic efficacy, and lessened side effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CNDs, TPP-CNDs, etc
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs with high fluorescence quantum yield of 17.84% (Fig. S1) was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CA and EDA [29]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a copper grid coated with super-thin carbon film, atomic force microscopy (AFM) on a silicon substrate, and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used to demonstrate the morphology of CNDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the mean particle diameter, height range, and average hydrodynamic particle size of CNDs are 1.80 ± 0.10 nm, ~4.0 nm, and 130.84 nm (PDI = 0.305), respectively. The lattice spacing of CNDs is approximately 0.22 nm, which may be attributed to the (100) facet of graphitic structure [24], demonstrating that the portion of CNDs have graphitic structure. The composition and functional groups of CNDs were performed by elemental analysis (EA) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), as shown in Table S1 and Fig. S3, respectively. The element contents of C, H, N, and O are 48.89%, 5.06%, 13.58%, and 32.47%, respectively, and the empirical formula for a CND is approximately C4H5NO2. In the survey scan of CNDs (Fig. S3a), the three main energy peaks are at 528.43, 396.71, and 281.96 eV, corresponding to O(1 s), N(1 s), and C(1 s), respectively. The high-resolution C(1 s) spectrum (Fig. S3b) can be divided into five peaks at 288.58, 286.51, 285.57, 285.01, and 284.28 eV, which are associated with O-CO, C-O/CN, C-OH/C-O-C, C-C, and CC, respectively [24,25,31]. N-H (401.01 eV) and pyrrolic-like N (400.19 eV) are found in the N(1 s) spectrum (Fig. S3c) [24,32]. As depicted in Fig. S3d, the O(1 s) spectrum is dissociated into OC-O*, C-OH/C-O-C, and C-N-O, which are located at 532.89, 531.95, and 531.20 eV, respectively [26,33,34]. These abundant carboxyl, pyrrole, and amine functional groups on CNDs are contributed to the copolymerization of CNDs and other substances. The optical properties of CNDs were demonstrated by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in the red line of Fig. S4a, two characteristic absorption peaks at 342 nm and 236 nm originated from the excited defect surface states induced by the N heteroatom and the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain, respectively [25,33]. The black and blue lines in Fig. S4a are the PL spectra of CNDs, illustrating that the optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) peaks are 348 nm and 441 nm, respectively. When the λex of CNDs moved from 300 nm to 400 nm, the λem of CNDs remained invariable at ~442 nm (Fig. S4b), showing that CNDs mainly possess λex-independent PL behavior.

Triphenylphosphine (TPP) can facilitate many molecules and nanoparticles to perform membrane transduction and mitochondrial localization, so TPP derivative was utilized as the ligand to target mitochondria. The synthetic process of TPP-NH2 is depicted in Scheme S1 [30], and the 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) results for primary and final products are depicted in Fig. S5 and S6, respectively. In FTIR spectrum (red line in Fig. S6), the stretching vibrations of NH2 can be found at 3432 cm−1, signifying that -NH2 has been successfully conjugated with TPP. Subsequently, TPP-CNDs were synthesized between TPP-NH2 and CNDs by amide reaction (Scheme S2). The zeta potential of CNDs is − 3.29 mV (Fig. S7) by the presence of more -COOH/-OH groups than -NH2 groups on the surface of CNDs. After conjugation with TPP-NH2, the zeta potential of TPP-CNDs becomes 2.14 mV (Fig. S7) due to the condensation reaction between the -COOH of CNDs and the -NH2 of TPP-NH2. TEM and AFM images of TPP-CNDs are shown in Figs. 1a and 1b, demonstrating that the average size and height of TPP-CNDs are 2.0 ± 0.10 nm and ~5.0 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S2c, the mean hydrodynamic particle size of TPP-CNDs is 152.72 nm (PDI = 0.327). These results demonstrate that a little effect on the size and height of CNDs can be found by grafting TPP on the surface of CNDs. EA, FTIR, and XPS were also used to characterize TPP-CNDs. In Table S2, the elemental contents of C, H, N, P, and O are 45.71%, 5.39%, 10.95%, 13.84%, and 24.11%, respectively, and the empirical formula of a TPP-CND is C17H24N4P2O7. As seen in Fig. 1c, the peaks at 1554–1705 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO), 3272–3476 cm−1 (stretching vibration of N-H), and 1180–1403 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C-N) of TPP-CNDs illustrate the formation of amide (-HN-CO). Two unique peaks at 652–756 cm−1 and 3070 cm−1 result from the bending vibration of C-H in benzene ring coming from TPP. Moreover, the survey scan of TPP-CNDs shows five peaks at 132.16, 183.12, 281.98, 397.01, and 528.58 eV, which correspond to P(2p), P(2 s), C(1 s), N(1 s), and O(1 s), respectively (Fig. 1d). In the C(1 s) spectrum (Fig. S8a), five peaks at 288.05, 286.52, 286.10, 284.99, and 284.29 eV correspond to COOH, C-O/CN, C-P, C-C, and CC, respectively [24,25,31]. Three peaks at 401.19, 400.39, and 399.71 eV in the N(1 s) spectrum (Fig. S8b) are associated with N-H, pyrrolic-like N, and amido-N/C-N, respectively [24,32]. As shown in Fig. S8c, three functional groups OC-O*, C-OH, and C-O-C are located at 532.89, 531.97, and 530.89 eV, respectively [26,32,33]. More importantly, P(2 s) and P(2p) XPS spectra were found in the XPS survey scan, demonstrating that TPP-CNDs were successfully prepared. In the P(2p) spectrum (Fig. S8d), two peaks are observed at 133.33 eV (P2p 3/2) and 132.56 eV (P2p 3/2 P-(C6H5)3) [25]. All results confirm the successful conjugation of TPP on the surface of CNDs. In Fig. S9, compared with CNDs (Fig. S1 and S4b), both fluorescence emission and quantum yield of TPP-CNDs show only slight alteration. Therefore, TPP-CNDs were successfully prepared with good water solubility and can be used for further copolymerization.

The synthetic route for TPP-CNDs-S and TPP-CNDs-C are depicted in Scheme S3. TPP-CNDs was reacted with triphosgene in the presence of DIEA, followed by treating with bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide to generate TPP-CNDs-S in toluene (Scheme S3a) [3]. For comparison, an uncleavable linker (“C-C”) was employed in TPP-CNDs-C (Scheme S3b). CNDs-S was fabricated to demonstrate the function of TPP. Similarly, FTIR and XPS were used to confirm the successful preparation of TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S. As depicted in Fig. S10, two main peaks at 2857–2985 cm−1 and 1564–1705 cm−1 are ascribed to CH2 (stretching vibration) and CO (stretching vibration), respectively. In Fig. 1e, the XPS survey scan of TPP-CNDs-S shows seven peaks at 150.10, 161.10, 196.94, 224.02, 280.92, 397.40, and 528.26 eV, corresponding to P(2p), S(2p), P(2 s), S(2 s), C(1 s), N(1 s), and O(1 s), respectively. Five peaks at 288.25, 286.89, 286.28, 285.31, and 284.70 eV in C(1 s) of TPP-CNDs-S (Fig. S11a) correspond to O-CO, C-O, CO/C-P/CN, C-OH/C-N, and CC/C-S, respectively [25,33,34]. In the N(1 s) spectrum (Fig. S11b), two peaks at 400.35 and 399.85 eV are associated with pyrrolic-like N and C-N-C, respectively [26,32]. As shown in Fig. S11c, three peaks in O(1 s) at 532.51, 532.01, and 531.28 eV are respectively *O-C-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and C-N-O [26,32]. S(2p) XPS spectrum (Fig. S11d) has three peaks at 168.40, 164.66, and 163.55 eV, corresponding to S2p 1/2 C-S, S2p 3/2 C-S, and S2p 3/2 S-S, respectively [33]. The P(2p) peak in Fig. S11e depicts P2p 3/2 (133.33 eV) and P2p 3/2 P-(C6H5)3 (132.59 eV) motifs of TPP-CNDs-S [25]. For TPP-CNDs-C (Fig. 1f), four peaks at 529.32, 397.10, 281.62, and 129.50 eV are associated with O(1 s), N(1 s), C(1 s), and P(2p), respectively. The C(1 s) (Fig. S12a), N(1 s) (Fig. S12b), O(1 s) (Fig. S12c), and P(2p) (Fig. S12d) XPS spectra demonstrate that TPP-CNDs-C has the characteristic functional groups of C-P and C-C. The XPS survey scan of CNDs-S (Fig. S13a) has four peaks at 161.97, 281.62, 397.08, and 529.32 eV, respectively corresponding to S(2p), C(1 s), N(1 s), and O(1 s). All peak fitting spectra in Fig. S13b-e indicate that CNDs-S has the important functional groups of O-CO, C-N, N-H, and S-S. These results demonstrate that TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S have been successfully prepared. Compared with CNDs and TPP-CNDs, the average particle size (6.07 ± 0.12 nm, Fig. S14a), height range (~7.0 nm, Fig. S14b), and hydration particle size (177.66 nm, PDI = 0.25, Fig. S2c) of TPP-CNDs-S have increased, indicating that the surface grafting of bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide on TPP-CNDs has a great influence on its spatial size. Ultimately, the fluorescence spectra and quantum yield of TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S were investigated and are shown in Fig. S15, S16, and S17, respectively. Compared with CNDs, no significant changes can be found in the emission spectra of TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S, so the fluorescence properties of CNDs were not affected by the graft reaction. The quantum yields of TPP-CNDs-S (4.43%, Fig. S15c) and TPP-CNDs-C (4.63%, Fig. S16c) are lower than those of CNDs (17.82%, Fig. S1b), TPP-CNDs (14.27%, Fig. S9b), and CNDs-S (13.36%, Fig. S17c), which may be ascribed to the consumption of COOH and NH2 on the surface of CNDs. Successful preparations of TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S contribute to further drug copolymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, etc
ce:section.ce:para:
TPP-CNDs-S-CPT was acquired by the reaction of chloroformate of TPP-CNDs-S and CPT at room temperature ( Scheme 2) [3,35]. For comparison, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT and CNDs-S-CPT were prepared by the reaction of TPP-CNDs-C and CPT (Scheme S4a) and CNDs-S and CPT (Scheme S4b), respectively. FTIR was used to illustrate the successful preparation of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT. As depicted in Fig. 2a, two characteristic peaks at 1564 cm−1 (νC
O) and 1646 cm−1 (νC
O) are revealed in the FTIR spectrum of CPT. Similarly, two peaks at 1604 cm−1 (νC
O) and 1654 cm−1 (νC
O), 1564 cm−1 (νC
O) and 1646 cm−1 (νC
O), and 1565 cm−1 (νC
O) and 1647 cm−1 (νC
O) were found in the FTIR spectra of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT, respectively. A sharp peak at 1743 cm−1 and a broad peak at 1155–1335 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CPT are associated with νC
O and νC-O-C, respectively, which can be found in the FTIR spectra of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT. These peaks demonstrate that CPT has been successfully combined with TPP-CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-C, and CNDs-S. Furthermore, zeta potential measurements indicate that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT are negatively charged with the zeta potentials of − 16.1, − 29.7, and − 18.0 mV (Fig. S7), respectively, which are lower than that of CNDs (−3.29 mV, Fig. S7), illustrating that some positive amino groups on CNDs were coupled with CPT in TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT by bis(2-hydroxyethyl) disulfide or 1,6-hexanediol. The high negative potential may contribute to the ultra-high stability of these nano-DDSs under physiological conditions [36]. The fluorescence spectra of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (Fig. 2b), TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (Fig. S18a), and CNDs-S-CPT (Fig. S18b) are similar to that of CNDs (Fig. S4), and excitation-independent emission behavior is also observed from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CNDs, CPT, and TPP-CNDs-S-CPT are shown in Fig. 2c. The typical absorption peaks of CNDs are 236 nm and 342 nm (black line in Fig. 2c). Four peaks at 217 nm, 253 nm, 288 nm, and 357–373 nm can be found in the absorption spectrum of CPT (red line in Fig. 2c). The characteristic absorption peaks of CPT can be found in the absorption spectra of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (blue line in Fig. 2c), TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (black line in Fig. S18c), and CNDs-S-CPT (red line in Fig. S18c), demonstrating that CPT has been combined with CNDs successfully. So, the absorption peak at 282 nm can serve as the characteristic peak to calculate the drug-loading capacity. As shown in Fig. S18d and Table S3, CPT loading capacities of the obtained TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT are 64.6 wt%, 53.5 wt%, and 50.0 wt%, respectively; which are significantly higher than that of Lac-SS-CPT [19], MPEG-(TK-CPT)-PPa [20], polymer-CPT [21], and DEX-PDP-CPT [22] with 31 wt%, 10.1%, 5–10 wt% and 5.8 wt%, respectively ( Table 1), demonstrating that the as-constructed TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is an excellent nano-DDS. The fluorescence quantum yield of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (Fig. S19a), TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (Fig. S19b), and CNDs-S-CPT (Fig. S19c) is 56.51%, 24.92%, and 37.36%, respectively, which are higher than that of CNDs (17.82%, Fig. S1b), which can be attributed to the hyper-fluorescent substance of CPT (82.59%, Fig. S19d). More importantly, after CPT grafted on the surface of TPP-CNDs-S, the mean particle size and height range of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT significantly increase to 7.10 ± 0.10 nm (Fig. 2d) and ~10.0 nm (Fig. 2e), respectively; especially the average hydrodynamic particle size increases to 219.91 nm (PDI = 0.173, Fig. S2c), which perfectly fit the optimal size of nanomaterials to produce efficient EPR effect [37–39]. Therefore, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is an outstanding reagent for biological imaging and efficient enrichment in tumor.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CPT is higher than that of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, and CNDs-S-CPT since part of the fluorescence of CPT was quenched by CNDs. This means that fluorescence enhancement can serve as an important parameter to investigate CPT release behavior from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT or TPP-CNDs-C-CPT. To verify TPP-CNDs-S-CPT containing an activatable disulfide linker (S-S), the spectral properties of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT upon the addition of GSH were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 f, the fluorescence of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT at 433 nm gradually enhances by about 4.43-fold with the addition of GSH. The PL intensity reveals good linear relationship with GSH concentration in two wide ranges of 0.64–14.50 μM (Fig. S20a) and 19.43–165 μM (Fig. S20b) with the linear equations of F/F0 = 0.0431c(GSH) + 1.0204 (R2 = 0.9914) and F/F0 = 0.0114c(GSH) + 1.5902 (R2 = 0.9902), respectively, where F0 and F are the PL intensities of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. On the contrary, the comparison TPP-CNDs-C-CPT with alkane bonds (“C-C”) as linker exhibits almost no change of PL intensity after the addition of the equivalent GSH (Fig. 2 g). The different fluorescence spectroscopic response of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT to GSH demonstrates that the release of CPT from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is derived from the cleavage of the disulfide linker by biological thiols such as GSH (Scheme S5). Furthermore, the pH and incubation time of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and GSH were investigated in detail. As depicted in Fig. 2 h, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can remain stable and emit a weak fluorescence within the pH range of 3.0–10.0. After treatment with GSH (0.5 mM), the fluorescence at 433 nm gradually increases in the pH range of 3.0–7.0 and reaches saturation in the pH range of 7.0–10.0. These results illustrate that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be used as a GSH-induced theranostics nano-DDS under weak acidic physiological pH conditions. In addition, the effect of incubation time on the release behavior of CPT from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT by GSH is shown in Fig. S21a. At 0–80 min, the PL intensity of the mixture solution of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and GSH is gradually increased, proving that the release of CPT from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is a slow process, but it cannot be confirmed that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT has the sustained release property of CPT. When the incubation time is higher than 80 min, the PL intensity of the mixture solution of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and GSH remains unchanged, so 80 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time and further used in all experiments.

To investigate the possible mechanism and application of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT in real biological systems, the fluorescence response of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT with other biologically relevant analytes (abundant metal ions and other amino acids) was studied and the results are depicted in Fig. S21b. No important changes in PL intensity are found by treating TPP-CNDs-S-CPT with abundant metal ions and other amino acids. However, after TPP-CNDs-S-CPT was exposed under homocysteine (Hcy), 1,4-dithiothreitol (DTT), and cysteine (Cys), a fluorescence response similar to that of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT to GSH can be observed, which may be attributed to their thiol-containing structures. Nevertheless, the potential interference of Hcy, DTT, and Cys in the biological system can be ignored because of their relatively low concentration in contrast to the high GSH concentration in cytoplasm and mitochondria (1.0–15 mM) [3,40]. Therefore, the fluorescence of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT with its response to GSH can serve as a direct turn-on signal reporter for the release of CPT. It is well known that the photostability of the nano-DDS is significant for practical imaging application in vitro and in vivo [3,41]. Therefore, the PL intensities of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and GSH-treated TPP-CNDs-S-CPT upon continuous Xe lamp irradiation for 180 min were performed to demonstrate its photostability. As depicted in Fig. S21c, during the whole process, the relative PL intensities of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and GSH-treated TPP-CNDs-S-CPT are in the range of 94.13–99.22% and 98.75–102.96%, respectively. No obvious changes are observed in PL intensity, and GSH-treated TPP-CNDs-S-CPT reveals much better photostability than TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, demonstrating that GSH-treated TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be used as a remarkable theranostic reagent for in vitro and in vivo imaging.

Given that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be activated to release CPT, we studied its performance in different cells in vitro. The representative CCK-8 assays were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CNDs, TPP-CNDs, TPP-CNDs-S, CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, and free CPT against a series of human and mouse cancer and normal cell lines ( Fig. 3 and S22). The cytotoxicity of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT to cancer cells including HeLa, 4T1, A549, HepG2, and MCF-7 was comparable to that of the naked drug CPT with remarkable in vitro antineoplastic activity, while CNDs-S-CPT revealed slightly less cytotoxicity. For normal LO2 cells (Fig. 3c), the cytotoxicity of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and CNDs-S-CPT was lower than that of CPT, demonstrating that CPT can be specifically released from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and CNDs-S-CPT in cancer cells. On the other hand, CNDs, TPP-CNDs, CNDs-S, TPP-CNDs-S, and TPP-CNDs-C cannot bring about obvious toxicity to cancer and normal cells, illustrating that the inhibitory effect to cells is attributed to CPT, not the basis material CNDs. Obviously, CPT can be sufficiently released from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT by the cleavage of the disulfide linker upon the uptake by cancer cells; nevertheless, CPT cannot be released from TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, resulting in lower cytotoxicity, which is consistent with the spectroscopic results. Compared with CNDs-S-CPT, CPT was efficiently released from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT against mitochondria, which means that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT possesses higher cytotoxicity than CNDs-S-CPT, making TPP-CNDs-S-CPT an excellent therapeutic nano-DDS. Moreover, the naked drug CPT affords prominent cytotoxicity towards normal LO2 cell, whereas CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT with the same drug concentration show much lower cytotoxicity to LO2 cell. Definitely, CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can release activated CPT in cancer cells rather than normal cells, attributing to the much higher concentration of intracellular GSH in cancer cells than that in normal cells (Fig. 3) [3,40]. More importantly, CNDs and TPP-CNDs do not show cytotoxicity to either cancer cells or normal cells, again indicating that CNDs is an excellent drug carrier and can be used to prepare specific theranostic nano-DDS.

The uptake by cells and intracellular localization of CNDs, CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT by model HeLa, A549, HepG2, and LO2 cells were investigated by laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM). As depicted in Fig. S23, the morphologies of the four model cells are great, demonstrating that CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-S-CPT cannot exhibit distinct cytotoxicity under short incubation time and low accessible concentration. To demonstrate whether TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT can specifically target and selectively stain mitochondria, a commercially available mitochondria-specific staining fluorescent dye, i.e., Mito Tracker Deep Red (MTred), was used to co-stain all model cells with the selected nanomaterials. The cell images and colocalization results of three model cells are depicted in Figs. 3d-3f. Though comparing the location of the red fluorescence from MTred and the blue fluorescence from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, it is easy to see the conspicuous overlap between them. Pearson’s correlation coefficient (correlation R) was used to evaluate the colocalization effect, which is shown in Table S4. The correlation R between TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and MTred in HeLa, A549, and HepG2 is 0.86, 0.89, and 0.93, respectively. Similarly, the correlation R of HeLa, A549, and HepG2 between TPP-CNDs-C-CPT and MTred is 0.80, 0.87, and 0.89, respectively. However, no colocalization is observed between CNDs or CNDs-S-CPT and MTred with correlation R in the range of 0.21–0.30. These results illustrate that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT can specifically stain the mitochondria region, which is mostly ascribed to the successful modification of TPP, a recognized mitochondria-targeting moiety in many published reports [27,42,43]. As seen from the merged images, the region of blue emission in cells matches well with that of the red fluorescence, which also manifests that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT primarily distribute in mitochondria. These results verify that the obtained TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT exhibit universal and mitochondria-targetable properties for various cancer cells.

Furthermore, after the same concentration of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT was respectively incubated with HeLa, A549, HepG2, and LO2 for 12 h, the cell images were obtained and are displayed in Fig. S24. As can be seen from the first row of Fig. S24, HeLa cells incubated with TPP-CNDs-S-CPT emit stronger blue fluorescence than that of cells incubated with TPP-CNDs-C-CPT. The same phenomenon can be seen in two other models of cancer cells, i.e., A549 (the second row of Fig. S24) and HepG2 (the third row of Fig. S24). However, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT-stained LO2 cells and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT-stained LO2 cells can emit almost identical blue fluorescence (the fourth row of Fig. S24). These results may be attributed to the high concentration of intracellular GSH in cancer cells, which can break the “S-S” linker to release CPT from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, while CPT cannot be released from TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (vide supra). The release of CPT can dramatically enhance the blue fluorescence in cells. Normal cells have a low concentration of GSH, so very few CPT can be released from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT or TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, accounting for weak blue fluorescence. At the same concentration of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, three cancer cell lines, HeLa, A549, and HepG2, can emit stronger blue fluorescence than the normal LO2 cells, demonstrating that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is an excellent probe to differentiate cancer from normal cells. This line of evidence indicates that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is an excellent mitochondria-targeting nanotherapeutic prodrug.

Another crucial issue for the biomedical application of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is its potential long-term biological toxicity. Long-term biological toxicity was investigated in animal models (the healthy mouse) by assessing variations in blood chemistries, changes of blood cell parameters, spleen coefficient, body weight, and variations in the histological features of organs after administering the nano-DDS [44–46]. In vivo toxicity studies of body weight, spleen coefficient, organ indices, blood routine examination, and tissue slice were subsequently evaluated on mice after a 28-day post-injection of normal saline (NS, G1), TPP-CNDs-S (G2), CNDs-S-CPT (G3), TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (G4), and TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (G5) for five times by intravenous injection in tail. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the body weights of mice change very little, or not at all after being treated with NS, TPP-CNDs-S, CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, or TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, demonstrating that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT has no potential biotoxicity. At 28 days post-treatment, the spleen coefficients of both G4 and G5 groups are consistent with that of the G1 group but lower than that of the G3 group, as shown in Fig. 4b. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the organ index is defined as the ratio of the wet organ weight to body weight (g/g). No significant difference can be noted in the heart, spleen, lung, and thymus indices between the control groups and the experimental groups, while a little effect can be seen in liver and kidney, which is within acceptable limits, illustrating that multiple injections of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT rarely cause obvious toxicity and side effects on the organs. Subsequently, blood extracted from the mice was used for the investigation of routine hematology. Considerable indexes of WBC, RBC, PLT, HGB, MCHC, MCH, MCV, MPV, PDW, HCT, RDW, Lymph%, Mon%, and Gran% were all measured and are depicted in Fig. 4. No significant changes in blood cell counts (Figs. 4d-4q) can be seen after multiple injections of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT into the mice, revealing high biosafety and low blood toxicity of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT at our tested dose within 28 days [47–49]. More importantly, after different treatments for 28 days, histology analysis of the major organs stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) was performed to estimate the potential toxicity effects of the nano-DDS on organs, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4r. For the heart, spleen, and lung, no visible morphological changes can be found in the control and experimental groups. Except for NS (G1) and TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (G5), more extramedullary hematopoietic foci (yellow circle on the second row in Fig. 4r) in liver can be found in the G3 group compared to that in G2 and G4 groups, demonstrating that the immunity of the mice in G2, G3, and G4 groups significantly degenerates, while that in G1 and G5 group is still at optimal status. We can see marked atrophy of renal capsule space in the kidney (green circle on the fifth row in Fig. 4r) in G2, G3, and G4 groups, but not in G1 and G5 groups, indicating that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT results in no evident damage to the kidney. Comparing the thymuses of the G3 and G5 groups, local hemorrhage (white circle on the sixth row in Fig. 4r) can be found in G3 group, which may be explained by the targeting effect of TPP in TPP-CNDs-S-CPT to reduce damage on the thymus. The results of histology analysis fully verify that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT cannot cause noticeable cellular structure changes or organ damage in all major organs. In other words, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT at the tested dosage exhibits low biotoxicity. All the results manifest that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT has remarkable biocompatibility and ultra-low long-term biological toxicity.

Encouraged by the results of superior biocompatibility and pleasant in vitro therapeutic effect, we then evaluated the in vivo antitumor activity of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT on the 4T1 tumor-bearing xenograft mouse model. Tumor-bearing mice were treated once every 5 days for 2 or 3 cycles with NS, TPP-CNDs-S, CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, or CPT at a CPT-equivalent dose of 5 mg/kg for 16 days post-tumor inoculation (Fig. S25a). Characteristic images of the mice with tumors and excised tumors, tumor volume, inhibition rates of tumor (IRT), tumor volume change, and tumor weight are displayed in Fig. 5a-c and Fig. S25b-d, respectively. As expected, the TPP-CNDs-S-CPT-treated group achieved the most effective tumor inhibition after 16 days of treatment (three injections), which is comparable to the results of the CPT-treated group (black dotted line with double arrows) in Fig. 5c. The IRT of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT-treated and CPT-treated groups is 90.07% and 90.03% (Fig. S25b), respectively, demonstrating that CPT can normally release from TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and maintain high drug activity. The IRT of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT (90.07%) is higher than that of the CNDs-S-CPT group (67.99%), showing that the mitochondria-targeting capability of TPP is significant, which is conducive to improving CPT availability by the synergistic damage of DNA in nucleus and mitochondria. However, the tumors in control groups (NS, TPP-CNDs-S, and TPP-CNDs-C-CPT) kept growing during the treatment, and the average tumor volume increased up to 3.0, 3.8, and 2.9-fold that of the original one, respectively (Fig. 5c). The average tumor volume of the TPP-CNDs-C-CPT-treated group is 14.6-fold that of the TPP-CNDs-S-CPT-treated group (Fig. 5c), illustrating that CPT cannot efficiently release from TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, which is consistent with the higher IRT induced by TPP-CNDs-S-CPT compared to that of TPP-CNDs-C-CPT. Two mice in the CPT-treated group died after treatment with CPT via intravenous injection twice (Fig. 5d), so this group was dropped from further testing. As revealed in Fig. 5d, only CPT-treated group shows 75% survival rate, while other groups displays 100% survival rate, demonstrating that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT has lower toxicity in vivo than CPT. Except for the CPT-treated group, the body weights of all mice gradually enhance throughout the whole experiment (Fig. 5e), suggesting an apparent lack of systemic toxicity of the multiple injections of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, while CPT can result in toxicity in mice. Therefore, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT should serve as an effective nano-DDS for in vivo tumor therapy. General toxicity was evaluated by organ indices. Decrease of organ index implies organ shrinkage or functionally weakening, whereas an increase usually implies that the organ is congestive, intumescent or proliferation, or that function is strengthen [25,50]. As displayed in Fig. 5 f, no significant differences can be found in heart, lung, and kidney indices of the experimental and control groups, indicating that the multiple injections of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT cannot cause obvious toxicity or side effects on the heart, lung, and kidney. However, a remarkable effect was found in the liver, spleen (Fig. S25e), and thymus indices of the experimental and control groups. We can see that the TPP-CNDs-S-CPT-treated group had only diminutive side effects on the liver, spleen, and thymus compared to those experienced by the free CPT-treated group. Furthermore, apart from the free CPT-treated group, no abnormal clinical symptoms or behaviors were found in either the control group or the experimental groups during the entire study course. Taken together, these results implicate that the TPP-CNDs-S-CPT does not produce any apparent toxicity in mice.

To further gain insight into biosafety, blood samples of the mice were collected for the analysis of blood cells (WBC, RBC, PLT, HGB, MCHC, MCH, MCV, MPV, PDW, HCT, RDW, Lymph%, Mon%, and Gran%), as shown in Fig. S26. All fourteen indexes fell within the normal ranges in the control and experimental groups, except for the free CPT-treated group in the indexes of RBC, PLT, HGB, HCT, Lymph%, Mon%, and Gran%, revealing negligible in vivo blood toxicity of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT at the treatment dose [47–49], while the free CPT can cause nonnegligible blood toxicity. Furthermore, the main mice organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and thymus) and tumors were harvested for histological analysis and H&E staining to evaluate the toxicity of viscera and therapeutic efficacy, which are displayed in Fig. 5 g. No observable histological abnormalities, necrosis variation, pulmonary fibrosis, inflammation lesions, or hydropic degeneration can be seen in the heart, spleen, lung, and thymus from the mice treated with NS, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-C-CPT, or TPP-CNDs-S-CPT. For the mice treated by CPT, however, oedema of myocardial fibers (white circle), congestion of splenic sinus (blue circle), pulmonary hemorrhage (cyan circle), and nuclear fragmentation (magenta circle) were observed in the heart, spleen, lung, and thymus, respectively, demonstrating that free CPT has strongly toxic to viscera. For liver in all groups, a small number of tumor cells can be found in sinus hepaticus (black arrow); while necrosis and calcification of hepatocyte (yellow circle) and hyperplasia of connective tissue are discovered in the liver of the CPT-treated group. Punctate infiltrates of neutrophile granulocyte (white arrow) in the kidney can be found in all groups; meanwhile, major renal tubular dilation (green circle) is shown in the kidney of the CPT-treated group. In the control groups (NS-treated, TPP-CNDs-S-treated, or TPP-CNDs-C-CPT-treated), we can see the tightly packed tumor cells interspersed with a mass of stroma, while apoptotic tumor cells were rarely found. After treatment with CNDs-S-CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, or CPT, the histological features of tumors reveal important differences from the control groups accompanied by obvious tumor necrosis. An obvious hemorrhage (black circle) can be found in the CPT-treated group. Tumors treated with TPP-CNDs-S-CPT and CPT have dramatically lower tumor cytoarchitecture and more apoptotic cells than those treated with TPP-CNDs-C-CPT (Fig. 5b), which complied with the higher inhibition rates of tumor growth induced by TPP-CNDs-S-CPT or CPT than TPP-CNDs-C-CPT. These results firmly demonstrate that TPP-CNDs-S-CPT has negligible systemic toxicity and displays excellent in vivo safety and good antitumor effect. Consequently, the as-fabricated TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be used as a highly efficacious, clearable, and minimally toxic nano-DDS by targeting mitochondria (TPP-group) and controlling release (GSH-response) to achieve enhanced antitumor efficiency, thus potentiating its future application as an efficient therapeutic nano-DDS.

CNDs with its surface rich in carboxy and amino groups, was fabricated using citric acid and ethylenediamine as reaction precursors, which can covalently combine with a mitochondria-targeting ligand (TPP) to synthesize TPP-CNDs. Following this step, a kind of smart GSH-responsive TPP-CNDs-S was prepared and used as a nanocarrier to conjugate with CPT using a disulfide linker. That is, a specific theranostic nano-DDS (TPP-CNDs-S-CPT) based on CNDs combined with TPP and CPT was designed for targeting mitochondria to treat cancer cells in vitro and a tumor in vivo. The as-constructed nano-DDS has ultra-high cytotoxicity in cancer cell lines (HeLa, 4T1, A549, HepG2, and MCF-7) and ultra-low biotoxicity in white mice. TPP modification can facilitate the cellular uptake of TPP-CNDs-S-CPT, making TPP-CNDs-S-CPT localizes in mitochondria to improve targeting ability. Furthermore, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT is suitable for ultraprecise cellular imaging, especially with mitochondrial targeting. TPP-CNDs-S-CPT can be effectively activated by the high concentration of GSH in cancer cells or tumor-bearing mice. What’s more, compared with the naked drug CPT, TPP-CNDs-S-CPT exhibits higher tumor inhibition activity and lower toxic side effects to normal tissues. Consequently, our work demonstrates a promising CNDs-supported nano-DDS for particularly mitochondria-targetable and tumor-activatable drug delivery systems, providing guidelines for the construction of nano-DDSs to overcome the critical challenges of chemotherapy.

Xiaojuan Gong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zihan Wang: Investigation, Data curation.
